turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion today in case 14-9496, Manuel versus City of Joliet.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: According to the complaint in this lawsuit, Elijah Manuel was held in jail for seven weeks for a crime he did not commit. That happened because police officers fabricated evidence, to be specific, made up drug test results, in order to persuade a judge that there was probable cause to think Manuel had committed a crime and that he should be detained pending the trial. Eventually, but again not before seven weeks had passed, the fabrications were uncovered and all charges against Manuel were dismissed. He then brought this lawsuit to seek compensation for his time in prison. The question we address is whether Manuel can bring a claim based on the Fourth Amendment to challenge his pre-trial detention as unsupported by probable cause. We hold that he can. The Fourth Amendment guards against unreasonable seizures. A pre-trial detention is a seizure and it is unreasonable when based solely on false evidence rather than supported by probable cause. So, Manuel's claim fits the Fourth Amendment, and the Fourth Amendment fits Manuel's claim like hand and glove and we have stated that much in several prior cases. As we said in one, “The Framers considered the matter of pre-trial deprivations of liberty, most especially pretrial detention, and they drafted the Fourth Amendment to address it. The lower courts thought otherwise and dismissed Manuel's claim. They believed that once a judge made a finding of probable cause, even though that finding had nothing to support it, even though it was based on fake evidence, the Fourth Amendment fell out of the picture. We reject that view. Again, the Fourth Amendment prohibits government officials from detaining a person prior to trial without probable cause. Sometimes, detention can occur without any judicial involvement, as when police hold a person at the station house for an extended period. Sometimes, as in this case, a judge makes the order to send a person to prison to await the trial. Either way, there has to be probable cause. If there is not, the Fourth Amendment has been violated and the person can then obtain redress through a suit like this one. The parties also dispute a procedural question here, whether Manuel brought his claim within the applicable two-year statute of limitations. That depends on when the clock began to run. When the judge ordered Manuel to prison, or when he was released. But we do not resolve that issue. Because the Court of Appeals thought that Manuel could not bring a Fourth Amendment claim at all, it never considered whether that claim was filed on time. We try not to resolve issues until other courts have addressed them. So, we send that issue back to the Court of Appeals. Justice Thomas has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Alito has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Thomas joins.
